summary: Gruber argues that Apple's switch to Intel processors represents a strategic partnership, not a prelude to acquisition as Robert X. Cringely speculated. He dismisses the idea that Intel would buy Apple or that Steve Jobs would ever cede control, noting that Jobs sees himself as a company-founding visionary in the mold of Walt Disney, not a hired executive. The partnership is complementary: Intel gains an innovative hardware partner willing to break new ground, while Apple gains reliable chip supply and competitive performance. Gruber draws a parallel to how Apple built Mac OS X on Unix — giving up building everything from scratch to concentrate on what differentiates them. He acknowledges the deal could lead to a powerful alliance against Microsoft, but insists a merger would add nothing beyond what a symbiotic partnership already provides.
key_points: Steve Jobs would never accept a subordinate role in an acquisition — he sees himself as a company founder, not a hired CEO, making any buyout scenario implausible
Intel and Apple are natural complements: Intel needs an innovative partner to push chip usage beyond incremental PC upgrades, while Apple needs reliable supply and competitive performance
The Intel switch mirrors Apple's adoption of Unix for OS X — outsourcing the commodity layer to focus engineering on the user experience that differentiates them
Apple's previous PowerPC benchmarks weren't dishonest but were selectively timed to moments when they had a performance lead
The partnership could challenge Microsoft's dominance, but a full merger would only antagonize Intel's existing customers and add no real benefit over a symbiotic relationship
money_quotes: Jobs doesn't want to take over Disney — he wants to turn Apple into a Disney-esque company.
Jobs thinks of himself as a peer to Walt Disney, a visionary who creates something of his own.
Intel would like to see a bit less evolution and a bit more revolution in the way its chips are being used. Apple is that type of company.

By switching to a mostly industry-standard Intel architecture, Apple obviously has no chance of ever surpassing the PC industry in performance. But the other side of that argument is equally true: they'll never again fall behind, either.
In a nut: if you can't beat them, join them.
This doesn't mean Mac hardware will simply be 'a PC with a pretty case' any more than that Mac OS X is merely 'Unix with a pretty UI'.
In the end, it's the product that matters, not the underlying technology.
Cringely, as he often does, started with a good idea but took it one click too far, turning the pundit dial to 11 when 10 would have done just fine.
key_insight: The Apple-Intel partnership mirrors Apple's adoption of Unix — outsourcing commodity infrastructure to concentrate on the user experience that actually differentiates the product.
